name: Acceptance Testing

x.defectClass: Mismatches between delivered behaviour and agreed requirements / user expectations.
x.oracle: Acceptance criteria / requirements agreed with the customer or product owner.
x.automationLevel: Medium — often automated (Gherkin/FitNesse) but may include manual UAT.
blindSpots: Only checks specified scenarios; unspecified behaviour untested
Quality of criteria bounds the value
Not a substitute for unit/integration depth
x.cost: Medium–high — scenario authoring and environment realism.
x.lifecycleStage: System / pre-release; the contract-level "is it acceptable?" gate.

def: Testing that verifies the system meets agreed business / user acceptance criteria — the gate that decides whether the work is "done" and shippable.
problem: Code can pass developer tests yet still fail to deliver what the customer actually agreed to.
mechanism: Acceptance criteria (often written as Given/When/Then scenarios) are executed against the system end-to-end, ideally in a realistic environment. Passing all criteria signals the feature satisfies the contract; some organisations include manual user acceptance testing (UAT).
benefits: Validates real requirements, not just code
limits: Slow / brittle if end-to-end and UI-driven
failures: Acceptance tests duplicating low-level unit coverage
myths: "Acceptance tests replace unit tests" — they sit atop the pyramid, not instead of it
